summary: O'Neil reviews the 1977 film Network, arguing it was remarkably prescient about how television and corporate power would shape society. She notes the film's prediction of media as entertainment rather than truth-telling, and its depiction of corporate dominance over democracy, seem almost boring now because they've become our reality. The film's central tension between individual agency and corporate control resonates strongly today. O'Neil finds it both depressing and thought-provoking, praising how it balances satire with emotional authenticity across multiple subplots.
key_points: Network predicted the rise of entertainment-as-news and filter bubbles decades before Fox News and social media
The film argues television deals in illusion while viewers increasingly mistake it for reality
Corporate power has superseded national and ideological boundaries - 'the world is a business'
Despite our individual rebellions, we are all ultimately 'cowed by the power of money'
Effective communication requires balancing apocalyptic warnings with hope and calls to action
money_quotes: Anyone who wasn't alive in 1977 might not – probably cannot – appreciate how incredible it must have seemed back then. It might even seem boring to someone who is used to a world of Fox News and the internet's filter bubble.
We might have our own particular emotional and political issues and rebellions, but we are all cowed by the power of money.
Television is not the truth! Television is a goddamned amusement park.
We deal in illusion, man! None of it's true!
You're beginning to believe this illusion we're spinning here. You're beginning to think the tube is reality and your own lives are unreal.
There is no America. There is no democracy. There is only IBM and ITT and AT&T.
The world is a college of corporations, inexorably determined by the immutable by-laws of business.
Being consistently depressing and apocalyptic gets old, even if there's an element of truth to it. It's critical to balance that with hope about the power of individual action.
key_insight: Network's 1977 satire about corporate media control and manufactured reality has become so accurate that younger viewers might find it unremarkable rather than prophetic.
